literal: the doctrine that the effect (kārya) is real (sat) [in the cause]
invariant_definition: The doctrine that the effect pre-exists in the cause and is therefore real before its production (against *asatkārya-vāda* — the Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika view that the effect is genuinely new at production). Sāṅkhya and all Vedāntic schools accept some form of *satkārya-vāda* against the Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika *ārambha-vāda*. The schools differ on what kind of *satkārya-vāda* — *vivarta* (Advaita), real *pariṇāma* (Bhāskara, V, D), or *avikṛta-pariṇāma* (Vallabha).

per_school.definition: *Satkārya-vāda* is universally accepted in some form against Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika *asatkārya-vāda*. The dispute is internal to *satkārya-vādins* on what kind of pre-existence and what kind of production.
primary_loci: Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya 2.1.14ff (where Śaṅkara argues for satkārya-vāda en route to vivarta)

translator_note: 'Pre-existent-effect doctrine' or 'doctrine of real effect-in-cause' both acceptable. The doctrine has no Western analogue; Aristotelian potency-actuality is the closest distant relative.
